results.title: 10 Email Templates to Respond to Customer Complaints
results.content:
### 4. The Customer Is Getting Conflicting Answers

The website, store staff, and/or support reps aren’t on the same page, so the customer feels confused, frustrated — even misled. The agent’s job is to provide concrete information about policies, pricing, and/or product availability straight from the source and make clear the issue is being taken seriously. Here is a customer service email response sample for a customer who is getting conflicting answers.

Dear [First name],

I’m so sorry for the confusion and frustration this has caused. As much as we rely on technology and training to provide customers with consistent, up-to-date information, we deeply regret when breakdowns like this occur. [...] The agent’s first order of business: track the customer’s package. If it’s marked as delivered, ask the customer to confirm the address. Maybe the package was left in a different location than they’re used to, or was marked delivered but left on the truck – it happens. If the package is still in transit, try something like this.

Dear [First name],

I’m so sorry your order hasn’t arrived. I know how frustrating this must be.

I’ve tracked the package via [carrier], and it’s currently listed as “[status].” If you’d like to check on its progress, here’s the link you can use: [link]

Please contact me directly if your order hasn’t arrived within [time frame]. In the meantime, I will do everything I can to locate your package. [...] ### 10. The Customer Had Problems with the Delivery

Delivery issues can be a significant source of frustration for customers, and rightly so. Their anticipation of a product is met with disappointment, a situation that requires not just a prompt response but one that fully acknowledges and addresses their concerns. You’’ll want to write a response that combines urgency with empathy in these scenarios. Here’s an email example designed to effectively address delivery concerns, ensuring the customer feels heard and valued:

Dear [First name],

I apologize for the problems you’ve encountered with the delivery of your order. We understand how important timely delivery is, and it’s disappointing to hear we’ve fallen short.

results.title: Email design maker: Design beautiful emails for free | Canva
results.content:
How to design your email

## Your questions, answered

 Canva Email makes it simple to create engaging emails using our drag-and-drop editor, no coding or complex email tools needed. You get access to hundreds of professionally designed email templates, rich visual content, AI-powered tools, and HTML export. Plus, you can create all your marketing materials on Canva, making it easy to stay on-brand and removing the hassle of switching between multiple apps. [...] ### Magic Write™ for email

  Create email copies with Magic Write™, an AI writer & text generator powered by OpenAI.
 Canva AI

  ### Canva AI

  Tag @Canva in comments to apply updates or get suggestions to polish your email.
 Magic Resize

  ### Magic Resize

  Transform your existing marketing content into on-brand emails with Magic Resize.

## How to design your email

1. Launch Canva

   Click “Email” on the homepage. You can also click “Create” at the upper left-hand corner and type “Email” in the search bar.
2. Choose a template

   Pick an email design template that fits your needs, like a marketing or a newsletter email design template. Or start with a blank page.
3. Customize your email design [...] Add text and info to your design. Explore Canva’s library, insert graphics and shapes, and use your Brand Kit to make every element on-brand.
4. Send yourself a test email

   Once you’re happy with your email design, send a test email to yourself to check how the final version will look like. Make tweaks if necessary.
5. Export your email design

   Download your email design as an HTML file. Then, send it through your preferred platform. You can also export it straight to Gmail from Canva.

results.content:
AMP for Email support: No

Best for: Freelancers

Free trial: N/A

#### 5. TOPOL

“Beautiful emails, made easy”

TOPOL SampleSource: TOPOL

TOPOL is very similar to Unlayer. It offers a simple, drag-and-drop visual editor, pre-made templates, and reusable blocks. The difference between these two platforms is that TOPOL offers a paid version. (Very affordable, but still!) 

Their paid version includes support for dynamic content. You also get unlimited test emails with this plan. And again, like Unlayer you have the option to embed this tool as a plug-in for your existing platforms. 

At-a-glance features:

★ feature: Support for dynamic content (paid)

 A no-code, drag-and-drop visual editor
 Support for exporting HTML email templates
 Reusable content blocks [...] ★ feature: A version history of your email templates

 A no-code, drag-and-drop visual editor
 Support for exporting HTML email templates
 Projects to organize your email templates (paid)
 Integrations with email service providers (paid)

Best for: Freelancers, small businesses, and agencies

AMP for Email support: No

Pricing:

 Free version
 Business: $18 / month
 Agency: $29 / month

Free trial: No free trial of the paid versions.

#### 4. Unlayer

“Create beautiful emails, easily”

Unlayer SampleSource: Unlayer [...] ##### Jeni McGuigan, Email Designer at AWeber

#### Q: What should you look for in an email builder?

There are three main things your email builder should excel at: Simplicity, consistency, and integration.

Simplicity: An email builder should be easy enough for anyone to use the first time they try (not just coders). 

Consistency: It may seem obvious, but the email you build should look the same for anyone who opens it — in Gmail, Yahoo, Apple Mail, Outlook, or any other platform. Find an email builder that takes care of this for you.

Integration: Email builders should be able to connect to other services you love to use. For example, designing with Canva inside your email builder (like AWeber or Dyspatch) can make the process of creating beautiful emails easy and fun.
